network.overall_strategy: This network centers the literature-supported core route for prebiotic isoleucine: generation of HCN and the branched aldehyde 2-methylbutanal in H2S-containing or reduced-gas spark-discharge/surface environments, followed by Strecker synthesis to the aminonitrile and hydrolysis to racemic isoleucine. Hydrothermal chemistry is used conservatively as a feeder network for simple carbon and nitrogen carriers (formate, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, ammonia) and not as a direct demonstrated source of isoleucine, consistent with the literature that vent simulations have not produced isoleucine. Additional literature-grounded auxiliary branches include meteoritic delivery of 2-methylbutanal/amino acid products, PAH-ice UV photolysis producing isoleucine, and speculative-but-explicitly-labeled keto-acid routes using Bucherer-Bergs or reductive amination from 3-methyl-2-oxopentanoic acid only where strictly necessary to complete the network. The 10 pathways are therefore variants of a small set of experimentally supported endpoint chemistries, with redundancy focused on convergent hubs rather than invention of unsupported de novo carbon-skeleton assembly.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 to C1 reduced carbon on iron-sulfur phases.
reactions.reasoning: FeS-family minerals and greigite are widely invoked for vent CO2 reduction to formate and related C1 products in alkaline hydrothermal settings; this provides a conservative hydrothermal carbon feedstock, not a direct isoleucine step (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Russell & Hall, 2006; Martin & Russell, 2007).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal/FTT generation of acetaldehyde from reduced carbon feedstocks
reactions.mechanism: Fischer-Tropsch-type reduction of carbon oxides generates mixed low-molecular-weight aldehydes including acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The literature report notes Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on metal surfaces as a plausible source of branched aldehyde precursors, but not clean selective access to isoleucine precursors; accordingly this node is treated as a mixed-feed precursor source only (Pizzarello & Cronin, 2008; Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate formation from CO2/H2

conditions.other: alkaline vent analog; low-yield protometabolic alpha-keto acid chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated multicarbon fixation producing alpha-keto acid intermediates.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is commonly treated as a plausible central prebiotic metabolite and is specifically highlighted in the research as a relevant feeder to underground biosynthetic analogs, but not as a demonstrated direct route to isoleucine in vents; therefore this step is used only as network support (Springsteen et al., 2018; Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Surface spark-discharge production of HCN from reduced volcanic gases

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and sulfide mineral surfaces as collection/photochemical interfaces
conditions.other: electrical discharge in CH4/NH3/CO2/H2S/H2O or CH4/N2/NH3/H2O atmospheres; volcanic lightning; anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Atmospheric discharge generates cyanide that is trapped into surface waters and mineral films.
reactions.reasoning: HCN is a core product of Miller-type discharge chemistry and the Strecker mechanism identified by Miller as the dominant amino acid-forming route; Parker et al. showed H2S-rich volcanic discharge conditions strongly expand amino acid diversity including isoleucine (Miller, 1957; Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Surface spark-discharge/radical formation of 2-methylbutanal

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and sulfide mineral surfaces as radical/photochemical interfaces
conditions.other: volcanic spark-discharge, H2S-enhanced radical chemistry; branched aldehydes formed in complex mixtures

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge and sulfur-radical chemistry generate branched hydrocarbon/aldehyde mixtures including 2-methylbutanal through radical recombination chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research identifies prebiotic generation of 2-methylbutanal as the key bottleneck and specifically points to radical chemistry in spark discharges, especially under H2S-containing conditions, as the best-supported terrestrial source. This step is intentionally broad and mixture-based rather than over-specified because selectivity is unresolved (Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Pizzarello & Cronin, 2008).

reactions.name: Surface Strecker synthesis of isoleucine aminonitrile from 2-methylbutanal

conditions.other: imine formation followed by cyanide addition in aqueous surface pools

reactions.mechanism: 2-Methylbutanal condenses with ammonia to an imine, followed by nucleophilic cyanide addition to form the aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the dominant literature-supported route to prebiotic isoleucine. Strecker chemistry from 2-methylbutanal, NH3, and HCN is explicitly identified as the best-supported mechanism for isoleucine formation in discharge experiments and extraterrestrial parent-body chemistry (Miller, 1957; Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Pizzarello & Cronin, 2008).

reactions.name: Surface hydrolysis of isoleucine aminonitrile to racemic isoleucine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling or standing aqueous pools; racemic and diastereomeric products expected

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydrolysis proceeds through amide/carboxyl intermediates to the alpha-amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of Strecker aminonitriles is the accepted final step to amino acids in Miller-type synthesis. The research emphasizes that isoleucine is produced as racemic/isomeric mixtures and that alloisoleucine is co-produced (Miller, 1957; Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Bada & Schroeder, 1975).

reactions.name: Surface Strecker synthesis of alpha-aminobutyric acid as a lower-homolog control branch

conditions.other: aqueous Strecker chemistry in discharge-fed pools

reactions.mechanism: Propanal undergoes Strecker reaction with NH3 and HCN to form alpha-aminobutyric acid.
reactions.reasoning: The research explicitly identifies alpha-aminobutyric acid as a readily formed homolog in Miller experiments and meteorites, illustrating how the isoleucine problem is specifically one of branched-carbon precursor availability rather than general amino acid inaccessibility (Ring et al., 1972; Higgs & Pudritz, 2009).

reactions.name: Biochemical/protometabolic conversion of pyruvate-derived network to alpha-ketobutyrate

conditions.other: protometabolic pool chemistry; speculative non-enzymatic analog of underground metabolism

reactions.mechanism: Network-level carbon rearrangement/homologation within alpha-keto acid chemistry gives alpha-ketobutyrate from central metabolites.
reactions.reasoning: The report specifically highlights alpha-ketobutyrate as a biologically central branch-point intermediate and notes underground biosynthetic routes from glyoxylate plus pyruvate in modern metabolism as prebiotically suggestive, but not established as a direct prebiotic isoleucine route. This step is therefore explicitly speculative and used only to connect the literature-discussed keto-acid branch (Kim et al., 2020; Springsteen et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Biochemical/protometabolic assembly of alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate

conditions.other: non-enzymatic aldol/condensation analogue; unresolved in prebiotic chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Condensation and rearrangement of alpha-keto acid feedstocks generate the branched C6 alpha-keto acid precursor.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research explicitly identifies alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate as a plausible penultimate intermediate but emphasizes that its prebiotic formation remains unproven. This reaction is included only because the literature also discusses reductive amination and Bucherer-Bergs chemistry from this precursor as chemically demonstrated endpoints (Ter-Ovanessian et al., 2025; Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Surface Bucherer-Bergs conversion of alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate to isoleucine hydantoin
reactions.mechanism: Alpha-keto acid reacts with cyanide and ammonia to form a hydantoin intermediate via Bucherer-Bergs chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Bucherer-Bergs chemistry was demonstrated as a bridge between alpha-keto acids and amino acids in prebiotic settings and is described in the report as general, though not specifically demonstrated for isoleucine's keto acid precursor. Thus the endpoint chemistry is literature-grounded, while precursor availability remains speculative (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Surface hydrolysis of isoleucine hydantoin to isoleucine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling supports hydrolysis and accumulation

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic ring opening and conversion of the hydantoin intermediate yields the alpha-amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis is the expected terminal step of Bucherer-Bergs amino acid synthesis. Included here as a literature-consistent completion of the keto-acid branch, while preserving the caveat that access to mol_013 is unresolved prebiotically (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

pathways.name: Volcanic H2S spark-discharge Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Core literature-supported terrestrial route: discharge chemistry generates HCN and branched aldehydes, then aqueous Strecker chemistry yields isoleucine.

pathways.name: Reduced-gas spark Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Ring et al.-style reduced atmosphere discharge path feeding the same Strecker endpoint through the same convergent hubs.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal carbon feed to surface discharge Strecker route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal FTT chemistry supplies simple aldehydic feedstocks to surface discharge environments where the key branched aldehyde and isoleucine are formed.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal ammonia feed to surface Strecker route
pathways.description: Ammonia from reduced hydrothermal geochemistry joins surface-generated HCN and aldehydes in the canonical Strecker sequence.

pathways.name: Meteorite-parent-body branched aldehyde to terrestrial hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Extraterrestrial delivery of 2-methylbutanal, documented isotopically on meteorite parent bodies, enters terrestrial Strecker chemistry and hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Meteorite-delivered aminonitrile hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Amino nitrile formed off-world or in discharge mixtures is delivered or accumulated and then hydrolyzed in surface pools to isoleucine.

pathways.name: Protometabolic alpha-keto acid to Bucherer-Bergs route
pathways.description: Speculative metabolism-first branch: central alpha-keto acid chemistry yields the branched keto acid precursor, then Bucherer-Bergs gives isoleucine via hydantoin.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formate-assisted protometabolic route to Bucherer-Bergs endpoint
pathways.description: Hydrothermal C1 reduction feeds the alpha-keto acid branch, converging on the same Bucherer-Bergs endpoint chemistry.

pathways.name: Hybrid hydrothermal-to-surface-to-protometabolic route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate and surface cyanide chemistry intersect in the keto-acid/Bucherer-Bergs branch, illustrating cross-environment network density.

pathways.name: PAH-ice/photolytic auxiliary source integrated through target pool
pathways.description: UV/EUV photolysis of PAH-containing ices is a separate literature-supported source of isoleucine; in the terrestrial network this contributes directly to the shared surface isoleucine pool that then participates in prebiotic accumulation and peptide chemistry.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest core route conceptually because it matches the literature-supported endpoint chemistry: spark/discharge generation of HCN and branched aldehyde followed by Strecker synthesis and hydrolysis to racemic isoleucine. The main weakness is rxn_004, which is chemically underspecified and written incorrectly as NH3 -> HCN; literature supports HCN generation from reduced gas mixtures under electrical discharge, not direct unimolecular conversion of ammonia. rxn_005 is plausible as a shorthand for discharge/radical formation of 2-methylbutanal, especially under H2S-containing conditions, but it is still compressed relative to the actual radical network. Final Strecker and hydrolysis steps are well aligned with known prebiotic chemistry.
suggestions: Rewrite HCN formation as arising from CH4/N2/NH3/H2O or CH4/NH3/CO2/H2S/H2O spark-discharge mixtures rather than NH3 alone.
Explicitly represent H2S-containing discharge conditions for the best-supported isoleucine-producing setting from Parker et al. 2011.
Clarify that 2-methylbutanal formation is inferred from radical chemistry and/or aldehyde precursor networks rather than a simple acetaldehyde + HCN step.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: As a reduced-gas spark variant, this pathway remains close to known Miller-type chemistry and reaches the target through the correct endpoint mechanism. However, without explicit reduced gas inputs in the network, it relies on an oversimplified HCN generation node and an underspecified branched aldehyde generation step. Still, the final chemistry is sound and literature-consistent in broad outline.
suggestions: Add explicit atmospheric precursor molecules to support the spark-discharge interpretation.
Differentiate reduced-gas and H2S-rich variants in conditions, because literature indicates the H2S case is especially important for isoleucine detection.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This hybrid route captures the intended hydrothermal-to-surface flow, but the hydrothermal feeder step to acetaldehyde is weakly justified in the current form. Hydrothermal systems may generate reduced carbon species, and mackinawite has been linked to HCN reduction products including acetaldehyde, but direct CO2 + H2 -> acetaldehyde as a simple node is too compressed and not a literature-standard route for isoleucine precursor supply. The surface Strecker endpoint remains the pathway's strongest section.
suggestions: Replace direct hydrothermal acetaldehyde formation with a more literature-grounded reduced-carbon feeder sequence or explicitly note it as a generic FTT-like carbon supply step.
Maintain hydrothermal chemistry as feeder-only, consistent with the literature that vent systems have not demonstrated isoleucine production.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway attempts a protometabolic keto-acid route, which the literature identifies as speculative for isoleucine. The Bucherer-Bergs endpoint itself is plausible in principle if alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate exists, but the preceding steps generating alpha-ketobutyrate and then alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate are not established prebiotically in the provided literature. The pyruvate/formate to alpha-ketobutyrate step is especially problematic. As presented, this is not a strong literature-aligned route.
suggestions: Downgrade this route explicitly to speculative auxiliary status.
If retained, connect alpha-ketobutyrate to literature-discussed glyoxylate + pyruvate chemistry rather than pyruvate + formate.
Distinguish demonstrated Bucherer-Bergs chemistry from undemonstrated formation of the required C6 keto acid precursor.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is highly coherent if interpreted as meteoritic delivery of 2-methylbutanal followed by terrestrial Strecker/hydrolysis chemistry. The provided literature strongly supports extraterrestrial occurrence of the branched aldehyde precursor and meteoritic isoleucine-related chemistry. The only limitation is that meteorite delivery is not explicitly represented as molecule or reaction nodes in the stated network, so the pathway is more conceptual than graph-complete.
suggestions: Represent meteoritic delivery as an explicit input process if pathway-level completeness is desired.
Clarify whether delivered 2-methylbutanal enters directly into Strecker chemistry in surface pools.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Hydrolysis of a delivered aminonitrile to isoleucine is chemically sound and consistent with Strecker logic. However, like P5, the meteoritic input is not explicitly encoded in the graph. As a minimalist auxiliary route it is plausible, but it is not fully realized as a standalone graph pathway from existing network nodes unless aminonitrile delivery is treated as external supply to mol_008.
suggestions: Add explicit external-source annotation for meteoritic aminonitrile delivery.
Specify hydrolysis conditions and likely aqueous alteration context.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The keto-acid/Bucherer-Bergs route is the weakest major branch. Literature supports Bucherer-Bergs on alpha-keto acids in general, but not the prebiotic generation of isoleucine's specific keto acid precursor in the way encoded here. rxn_010 especially compresses multiple biosynthetically analogous transformations into a single unsupported protometabolic step. This branch is acceptable only as a clearly labeled speculative alternative.
suggestions: Anchor alpha-ketobutyrate production to glyoxylate + pyruvate literature if possible.
Avoid implying experimental demonstration for nonenzymatic alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate assembly unless evidence is cited.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Hydrothermal formate production is one of the more plausible feeder reactions in the network, but using formate to support a route to alpha-ketobutyrate and then to isoleucine remains highly conjectural. The presence of a sound initial hydrothermal carbon-fixation step does not rescue the speculative middle of the pathway. Final Bucherer-Bergs/hydantoin hydrolysis chemistry is plausible if the keto acid is granted.
suggestions: Keep formate as a network hub for carbon supply, but do not overstate its direct role in isoleucine-specific carbon-skeleton assembly.
Separate experimentally grounded feeder chemistry from speculative downstream keto-acid elaboration.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This mixed route demonstrates environmental interconnection, but chemically it combines several of the least secure steps in the network. It inherits the problems of oversimplified HCN and branched aldehyde generation as well as unsupported keto-acid elaboration. It is connected topologically, but not strongly convincing mechanistically.
suggestions: Favor the direct Strecker branch over a hybridized route that mixes unsupported middle steps.
Use hybrid pathways only where each handoff is independently literature-supported.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The literature does mention PAH-ice UV photolysis as an auxiliary source of isoleucine, but this pathway is not actually represented by a dedicated reaction in the graph. Mapping it through rxn_007 is not valid. As a claimed pathway, it is unsupported by the submitted network structure even though the research basis exists externally.
suggestions: Add an explicit PAH-ice photolysis reaction node if this pathway is to be counted.
Do not claim a one-step integrated source unless represented in the molecule/reaction graph.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is topologically adequate and connected, with sufficient molecule and reaction counts and several genuine hubs, especially HCN, NH3, pyruvate, and the aminonitrile/target region. It also succeeds at a high-level literature insight: the direct hydrothermal origin of isoleucine is avoided, while spark-discharge Strecker chemistry is treated as the main productive route, which strongly aligns with Ring et al. 1972 and Parker et al. 2011. The network also appropriately notes that keto-acid/Bucherer-Bergs alternatives are more speculative. However, the chemistry is weakened by several compressed or incorrect reaction encodings. Most notably, HCN generation as NH3 -> HCN is not chemically defensible as written, and branched aldehyde formation from acetaldehyde + HCN is too schematic to count as a well-supported elementary step. The protometabolic branch to alpha-ketobutyrate and alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate is the main literature-misaligned section: Bucherer-Bergs chemistry is plausible only after the specific keto acid exists, but its prebiotic formation is not established here. Environmental interconnection is present, but some hydrothermal feeder reactions are overgeneralized, and the summary provided does not specify required mineral agents for hydrothermal and surface reactions, which is a framework-level omission. Literature alignment is moderate-to-good for the dominant Strecker concept, good in recognizing hydrothermal limitations and meteoritic support, but weaker in execution for explicit reaction-level fidelity. Overall, this is an acceptable but not approval-level network.
